title: The Silent Dowry
blurb: Her wedding day explodes in scandal. The groom flees. The benefactor vanishes—except Lila knows who really funds Monterey’s glittering world. One ledger holds the truth. One vote could ruin her.
comparable: For fans of *The Last Mrs. Parrish* by Liv Constantine, *The Seven Husbands of Evelyn Hugo* by Taylor Jenkins Reid

chapters.text:
Chapter 1

The Monterey Bay Lodge ballroom smelled of lemon polish and roses tied too tight. Lila stood at the center of the polished floor, her heel clicking against the parquet as she turned. The grand piano’s lid remained open, its black keys catching the afternoon light like a row of teeth.

Marisol Vega adjusted the place cards on a linen-draped table. “You’re doing it again,” she said.

Lila didn’t look up from the sheet music. “Doing what?”

“Reciting the vows in your head.” Marisol plucked a carnation from the centerpiece, snapped off a wilted petal. “You’re supposed to say them out loud.”

Lila exhaled through her nose. “I know how to rehearse a wedding.”

“Not like this, you don’t.” Marisol tossed the carnation aside. It landed on the carpet without a sound. “Clayton’s family is flying in tomorrow. You need to sound sure when you say ‘I do’ in front of them.”

“I will be sure.” Lila smoothed the front of her rehearsal dress—ivory silk, unstructured, expensive. No one would guess it cost more than the bridesmaid dresses combined. “I’ve been rehearsing since I was six.”

Marisol’s pen hovered over her clipboard. “That’s not what I meant.”

The piano lid’s brass clasp glinted. Lila’s fingers twitched. She hadn’t noticed it before. She reached out, traced the engraved initials on the inside of the lid: M.V.C.

Her mother’s initials.

Lila’s throat tightened. She closed her eyes. Ten years since the phone call in Paris, the one that made her father cancel the trip, the one that brought her home to find the house empty except for the note. Ten years since she learned the truth about the mounting debts, the hollowed-out shell of their fortune, the way her mother had chosen silence over surrender.

“Lila.” Marisol snapped her fingers. “Stay present.”

Lila opened her eyes. “I am.”

Marisol wasn’t fooled. She never was. That’s why Lila kept her close. “Then say the vows. Pretend I’m the officiant.”

Lila cleared her throat. “Clayton James Reeves, do you take me, Lila Voss Cortez—”

“With all your assets and liabilities,” Marisol interrupted.

Lila’s lips thinned. “—to be your lawfully wedded wife?”

Marisol stepped back, arms crossed. “Better. Now say it like you mean it.”

Lila turned, squared her shoulders to the empty chairs. The chandeliers cast fractured light across the floor. “In sickness and in health.” Her voice steadied. “For richer, for poorer.”

Marisol’s pen scribbled on the clipboard. “Good. You’re not trembling.”

Lila didn’t answer. She drifted toward the piano instead. Ran her gloved fingertips along the edge of the closed keyboard cover. The lid above her head bore the same initials—M.V.C.—carved into the wood.

“Who else knows?” Lila asked softly.

Marisol didn’t look up. “Knows what?”

“This.” Lila tapped the lid. “The initials.”

Marisol finally glanced over. “No one’s used this room since the Winter Gala. I checked the ledger myself.”

“Not recently. Ever.”

Marisol’s tone softened. “It’s just a piano, Lila.”

“It’s not.” Lila’s glove snagged on a loose thread near the hinge. She tugged it free, examined the frayed edge. “It’s proof.”

Marisol stepped closer. “Proof of what?”

Lila folded the glove over the thread, tucked it into her palm. “That I used to listen.”

A breeze from the ocean rattled the French doors. Salt air crept into the ballroom. Marisol crossed to close them, but Lila didn’t move. She stared at the piano, at the initials that had once belonged to a woman who taught her to notice what others ignored.

A sound made her turn.

The front doors of the lodge creaked open. A delivery man in a blue uniform wheeled in a crate of champagne. He paused, nodded at Lila. “Ms. Cortez? Sign here.”

Marisol took the clipboard. Lila watched the delivery man’s gloved hands—left thumb slightly calloused. Odd place for a callous. He pressed the pen into her palm; his fingers lingered a second too long.

Then his sleeve shifted.

A stitch of white thread glinted at his wrist—a monogram.

M.V.C.

Lila’s glove slipped from her fingers. It hit the floor just as the piano lid slammed shut above her, the hinge catching her glove’s cuff. She jerked back. The lid bounced once, then settled.

Marisol jumped. “What the hell—?”

Lila stared at the glove, now trapped under the lid’s weight. The thread from the hinge had snagged the lace at her wrist. She tugged, but the glove wouldn’t budge.

The delivery man cleared his throat. “Rough mechanism. Happens sometimes.”

Lila didn’t take her eyes off the glove. The lace was torn where the thread had caught it. The monogram on the man’s wrist matched the one on the piano. M.V.C.

Her mother’s initials.

Her blood turned cold.

Marisol crouched, tried to lift the lid. “This is ridiculous.”

Lila’s fingers trembled. She folded her ungloved hand into a fist. “Get off the floor, Marisol.”

“What—?”

“Now.” Lila’s voice was low, sharp. “The lid could fall on you.”

Marisol stood, backed away. The delivery man backed toward the door, palms raised. “I didn’t touch it.”

Lila didn’t answer. She reached out, braced one hand on the piano’s side, the other on the lid. The glove was ruined. The lace shredded. The monogram—

She saw it clearly now.

Not M.V.C.

M.V., then a small C beneath, almost like an afterthought.

Her own initials. And a second set.

*Maman. Who taught me to listen.*

She blinked. The thread in the delivery man’s glove was the same. Not a monogram. A ledger.

She knew this stitch pattern.

She’d paid for it.

The delivery man’s smile faltered. “You okay, Ms. Cortez?”

Lila’s pulse hammered in her throat. She didn’t answer. She lifted the lid just enough to free the glove, then let it drop without another word.

The delivery man backed out, mumbled an apology, vanished through the doors.

The ballroom was silent again.

Marisol exhaled. “That was weird.”

Lila didn’t speak. She slid her glove back on, smoothed the fabric over her fingers. Her palm was damp.

Marisol noticed. “You’re shaking.”

Lila slid her unbound hand into her pocket. “Get me the ledger.”

Marisol didn’t ask which ledger. She knew.

“He’s not going to give it up easily.”

Lila walked to the French doors, stared at the Pacific. “I know.”

Marisol joined her. “You think he knows?”

Lila didn’t answer. The ocean stretched endless and blue. Somewhere beneath it, a ledger page floated, waiting to surface.

Marisol checked her watch. “Rehearsal’s over. You’re supposed to meet Clayton for dinner at seven.”

Lila nodded. “Tell him I’m delayed.”

Marisol hesitated. “You want company?”

“No.”

Marisol left. The doors clicked shut.

Lila stayed by the window. The piano stood in the corner, lid closed. The glove on her left hand was ruined. The thread from her glove matched the thread from the delivery man’s cuff.

She flexed her fingers. The lace was irreparable.

She’d need a new glove by tomorrow. Something black. Something unobtrusive.

A mask.

Chapter 2

The Monterey Bay Lodge’s private dining room smelled of candle wax and seared scallops. Lila adjusted the linen napkin in her lap, fingers tracing the embroidered crest of the lodge. The table was set for twelve—Clayton’s parents, the mayor, Conrad Holloway III and his wife, Tomas with an empty seat beside him. The chandeliers cast golden pools on the white tablecloth, making the crystal glasses flare like captured fire.

Clayton leaned in, voice low. “You look like you’re about to give a eulogy.”

Lila smiled, but it didn’t reach her eyes. “Just thinking about the speech.”

Clayton chuckled, swirling his Bordeaux. “You’ve got nothing to worry about. My parents adore you.”

Tomas cleared his throat from the head of the table. He adjusted his cufflinks, silver monograms catching the light. “Lila, darling, you’ll want to practice your toast. Conrad prefers brevity.”

Conrad Holloway III sat two seats down, his salt-and-pepper beard trimmed to a severe line. He didn’t look up from the menu. “Speech implies a performance. Tonight is about gratitude.”

Lila’s grip tightened on her napkin. She folded it neatly, placed it beside her plate. “Then I’ll keep it short.”

The waiter arrived with the first course: chilled pea soup in wide, shallow bowls. Lila dipped her spoon, the cool liquid sliding across her tongue. The soup was perfect—bright, balanced. Holloway family money. Again.

Conrad raised his glass. “To the happy couple. May your union bring prosperity to Monterey.”

Clayton grinned, raised his own glass. “Hear, hear.”

Lila lifted her glass, clinked it against Clayton’s. The crystal chimed, too sharp. The others followed, their glasses chiming in a brittle chorus.

Conrad set his glass down. “Though I do wonder, Clayton, how long you’ll keep your bride once you learn the full extent of her generosity.”

The table went still. The soup spoons hovered mid-air. Clayton’s smile faltered. “Excuse me?”

Conrad’s eyes flicked to Lila, then back to Clayton. “Did you know your fiancée has been funneling Holloway family funds into her little pet projects? The Estero restoration? The scholarships at the maritime academy? All under her own name, of course. Very generous. Very convenient.”

Lila’s pulse hammered in her throat. She didn’t blink. Didn’t flinch. She set her spoon down, placed her hands in her lap. “Conrad.”

Conrad held up a hand, silencing her. “No need to deny it. The ledgers don’t lie.” He reached into his breast pocket, pulled out a folded document. “I took the liberty of printing a few pages. Circulated them among the board, just to be thorough.”

He slid the pages down the table. Tomas took them, his expression unreadable. He unfolded one, scanned it, then passed it to Clayton.

Clayton’s face darkened as he read. He looked up at Lila. “Is this true?”

Lila didn’t answer. She stared at the pea soup, the green swirled into artful spirals on the white porcelain.

Conrad leaned back, satisfied. “Eight million dollars. All under the guise of Voss Cortez philanthropy. How many of your so-called donations do you think will dry up once people realize the money wasn’t yours to give?”

Tomas cleared his throat. “Conrad, perhaps this isn’t the time.”

“When is the time?” Conrad’s voice was a blade honed to a whisper. “The Harbor redevelopment vote is in forty-three days. You don’t think people should know who’s really pulling the strings?”

The waiter returned, carrying a tray of scallops. He hesitated, sensing the tension. Lila shook her head infinitesimally. He backed away.

Clayton stood, chair scraping against the floor. “Lila.”

She finally looked at him. His face was a mask of betrayal and confusion. “Tell me it’s not true.”

Lila exhaled through her nose. “Sit down, Clayton.”

“No.” He tossed his napkin onto the table. “I want to hear you say it.”

The room held its breath. The chandeliers hummed. Lila folded her hands on the table. “It’s complicated.”

Conrad barked a laugh. “Complicated? You’ve been siphoning my money for years. That’s theft, not complication.”

Clayton’s fists clenched at his sides. “You used Holloway money? For what?”

Lila didn’t answer. She glanced at the empty seat beside Tomas. The one reserved for Diego.

Conrad smirked. “Cat got your tongue, Lila? Or are you just afraid of the truth?”

Diego Rivas stood in the doorway.

He was taller than Lila remembered, broader in the shoulders. His dark hair was windswept, his jaw shadowed with stubble. He wore a faded navy sweater, the sleeves pushed up to his elbows. His eyes locked onto Lila’s, unreadable.

The room seemed to exhale around him.

Clayton noticed. “Who the hell is that?”

Diego stepped into the room, hands in his pockets. “Evening, everyone.”

Conrad’s smirk faded. “Rivas. What are you doing here?”

Diego didn’t look at him. He kept his gaze on Lila. “Heard there was a party. Thought I’d crash.”

Tomas’s face darkened. “Diego. This isn’t—”

“—appropriate?” Diego finished. “When has that ever stopped me?”

Clayton’s jaw tightened. “Lila, who is this?”

Lila stood. The chair legs scraped against the floor. “This is Diego Rivas. Old family friend.”

Conrad’s eyes gleamed. “Ah. The marine biologist. The one who got himself disgraced.”

Diego’s hands flexed at his sides. “Conrad.”

“Careful, boy. You’re not in your wetland now.”

Diego ignored him. He stepped closer to Lila. “You look like you could use a breath of fresh air.”

Lila didn’t move. The room was a pressure cooker, ready to burst. Conrad’s documents lay scattered across the table like fallen leaves. Clayton’s face was a storm cloud. Tomas’s eyes darted between them all, calculating.

Diego held out his hand. “Come on, Lila. Before this gets uglier.”

She looked at his hand. Then at him. His palm was calloused, the skin rough from years of fieldwork. Years since she’d seen him.

She placed her hand in his.

His fingers closed around hers, warm and firm. He didn’t pull her toward the door. Didn’t rush her. Just stood there, waiting.

Lila turned to the table. “Excuse me.”

Clayton’s voice cracked like ice. “Where do you think you’re going?”

She didn’t answer. She let Diego lead her out, the heavy oak door swinging shut behind them.

The hallway was quiet, dimly lit. The sound of their footsteps echoed against the polished floor.

Diego didn’t speak until they reached the grand foyer. The lodge’s chandelier cast fractured light across the marble tiles.

“You want to tell me what the hell that was?” he asked.

Lila crossed her arms. “You heard.”

“Yeah.” He scratched the back of his neck. “I heard Conrad just tried to ruin you in front of the whole town.”

“He thinks he did.”

Diego’s eyes narrowed. “What’s that supposed to mean?”

Lila shook her head. “Not here.”

Diego stepped closer. She could smell the salt on him, the faint tang of seaweed. “Why not? You got a safe word? A secret hideout?”

She held his gaze. “The mansion.”

Diego’s expression flickered—something dark, something old. “Your place? That rotting relic?”

“Yes.”

He exhaled through his nose. “Fine. But we’re taking my truck.”

She followed him outside. The lodge’s porte-cochère provided scant shade from the setting sun. Diego’s truck was parked crookedly in the circular drive, the bed filled with gear—waders, a coiled rope, a duffel bag stained with salt.

He tossed the duffel into the cab, then turned to her. “You’re really gonna let him think he won?”

Lila looked back at the lodge. The windows glowed with golden light. Someone was laughing inside.

“No,” she said. “I’m not.”

Diego studied her face. Then, without another word, he opened the passenger door.

She climbed in.

The door slammed shut behind her. The engine roared to life.

Diego glanced at her as he shifted into drive. “You’re bleeding.”

Lila touched her lip. Her fingertip came away red.

She hadn’t even noticed.

Chapter 3

The Voss family mansion sagged under the weight of salt air and neglect. Lila’s heels clicked against the warped hardwood as she crossed the foyer, her breath shallow. The air smelled of mildew and old mahogany, the scent of a fortune built on rot.

Tomas stood in the arched doorway to the library, his briefcase balanced on a stack of moth-eaten ledgers. His cufflinks glinted under the stained-glass dome above. “You’re burning everything, not just watching it go up in smoke.”

Lila knelt by the fireplace, the last embers still glowing. She fed the ledger pages into the flames one by one. The paper curled black at the edges, then flared bright before collapsing into ash. “It’s called damage control.”

“Damage?” Tomas stepped forward, his polished shoes sinking slightly into the rotting rug. “You’ve lost the game, Lila. Accept it.”

She didn’t look up. “I didn’t lose anything.”

“You let Conrad expose you in front of the mayor, the board, your fiancé.” The briefcase thudded onto the side table. “You humiliated yourself. Him. Your family name.”

The fire popped. Spots of ash drifted upward, catching on the soot-stained stone. “Family name is already mud. You made sure of that.”

A muscle twitched in Tomas’s jaw. He pulled a folded check from his breast pocket. “I’ve taken the liberty of drafting a settlement. Generous, considering. You walk away, sign a nondisclosure, and ten million goes into an account in your name.”

Lila didn’t reach for the check. “I’m not signing anything.”

“You will.” His voice dropped, smooth as oil. “Otherwise, I’ll have no choice but to remind the board exactly how you’ve been laundering Holloway money. Not just embezzling—*fraud*. That’s prison, Lila.”

She tossed another page onto the fire. The flames hissed. “Try it.”

Tomas’s thumb rubbed the edge of the check. “This isn’t a negotiation. It’s surrender.”

“Then surrender all you want.”

He exhaled through his nose, clipped the check back into his pocket. “Fine. But don’t come crawling back when the real world chews you up.” He turned toward the door.

Lila watched the fire. Watched him leave.

The front door slammed. The house trembled.

Silence rushed in.

Then—a floorboard creaked on the porch.

Lila froze.

Diego Rivas stood in the doorway, silhouetted against the late afternoon sun. His shirt was damp at the collar, the fabric clinging to his shoulders. He held a worn leather ledger in one hand, the spine cracked with age. He didn’t smile. Didn’t speak.

She stood slowly, brushing ash from her palms. “You followed me.”

“Not hard.” He stepped inside, the screen door slapping shut behind him. “This place sticks out like a sore thumb. Even from the road.”

Lila’s pulse jumped. “What are you doing with that?”

He flipped the ledger toward her, letting her see the embossed initials on the cover: HRV. Holloway-Rivas Ventures. His thumb traced the binding. “Found it in my dad’s safe. Funny thing—every page lists a donation. Scholarships. Wetlands. A maritime academy. All signed *L. V.*”

Her throat tightened. “You shouldn’t be here.”

“Shouldn’t I?” He tossed the ledger onto the dining table, where it landed with a thud that sent dust swirling. “After ten years of radio silence, you show up at some rich man’s dinner table with my dad’s protégé walking out hand in hand, then vanish into this mausoleum? Yeah, I’m gonna follow.”

Lila stepped closer, her heels clicking again. “It’s not what you think.”

“Then tell me what it is.” His voice was low, rough. “Because from where I stood, it looked like you got caught. And now you’re burning evidence.”

She reached for the ledger. Diego moved it out of her grasp.

“Don’t touch it.” His eyes locked onto hers, dark and unreadable. “Not until you explain.”

Lila exhaled through her nose. The firelight flickered across his face, carving shadows into the angles of his cheekbones. He looked older. Harder. Not the boy who used to sneak her father’s whiskey down by the docks.

“I wasn’t stealing from Holloway,” she said.

“Then what *were* you doing?”

“Fixing what your father helped break.”

Diego’s jaw tightened. “My father? Or *you*?”

The words hit like salt in a wound. Lila turned away, walked to the window. The glass was wavy, distorted. Beyond it, the Monterey Bay glinted under the dying sun.

“You remember my mother’s piano?” she asked.

“Faded Steinway in the west parlor. Tuned once a year whether it needed it or not.”

“She played it the night she died.” Lila’s fingers curled into the windowsill. “Do you know what song?”

Diego didn’t answer.

“‘Moonlight Sonata.’ Third movement.” She turned back to him. “I heard it through the floorboards. I thought she was practicing. I thought—” Her voice cracked. She stopped.

Diego’s throat worked. “You were a kid.”

“I saw her afterward. In the study.” Lila swallowed. “She left a note. Just initials. M.V. At the bottom. And a glove. Holloway glove. Delivered that morning.”

Diego’s face went still. “You never told me that.”

“Because I didn’t know what it meant.” She stepped closer. “Until I found the ledgers. The ones Tomas just helped you ‘find.’ They show Holloway Shipping taking loans from my family’s bank—the same bank your father chaired. Then calling them due when the shipping business collapsed. My father couldn’t pay. Neither could my mother. And Conrad—Conrad made sure the house went to auction before sunrise.”

Diego’s hands clenched into fists. “He wouldn’t.”

“He did.” Lila’s voice was quiet, steady. “Every page in those ledgers shows Holloway money being redirected. Not stolen. *Returned.* With interest. To the people they ruined. To the wetlands they drained. To the kids who deserved an education.” She gestured at the ledger on the table. “That book proves it. And now you’ve got it.”

Diego stared at the ledger. Then at her.

She held his gaze. “So yes, I burned the rest. Because if Conrad sees even one page of this, he’ll spin it. Say I’m a thief. Say I’m unstable. Say I don’t deserve to walk Monterey’s streets. And he’ll be right—about the unstable part. Because I will burn his whole empire down before I let him win.”

Diego didn’t move. Didn’t speak.

The clock on the wall ticked, loud in the hollow house.

Then he picked up the ledger. “You’re not burning anything else today.”

Lila’s breath caught. “What are you doing?”

He tucked the ledger under his arm. “Taking this back where it belongs.”

“You can’t.”

“Watch me.” He walked toward the door.

Lila moved, cutting him off. “Diego.”

He stopped. Just inches from her. Close enough she could smell the salt on his skin again. Close enough to see the gold flecks in his brown eyes—freckles of sunlight she’d forgotten.

“You don’t understand,” she said. “That ledger isn’t just paper. It’s proof. Proof Conrad can’t bury. Proof I’m not the villain in his story.”

“And what happens when you march into his boardroom with it?” Diego’s voice was rough. “You think he’ll fold? He’s been playing this game longer than you’ve been alive.”

Lila lifted her chin. “I don’t need him to fold. I need him to *see*.”

Diego held her gaze. The seconds stretched. The fire popped in the hearth.

Then he exhaled, sharp. “You’re impossible.”

“So are you.”

He didn’t step back. She didn’t move. The air between them thickened, charged like storm clouds over the bay.

Diego broke first. He shifted the ledger to his other hand, brushing against her fingers. A touch accidental. Or maybe not.

Lila’s breath hitched.

He didn’t acknowledge it. Just said, “I’m keeping this. But I’ll give you twenty-four hours to come up with a plan. Because you’re not walking into Holloway Shipping alone.”

She nodded once. “Deal.”

He turned toward the door.

“Diego.”

He paused.

She looked at him—the man who’d once built forts with her in the dunes, who’d taught her to bait a hook and tie a bowline, who’d disappeared without a word and left her drowning in a world of silk and secrets.

“Thank you,” she said.

He didn’t smile. Didn’t soften. Just gave a single nod, opened the screen door, and stepped into the dying light.

The door swung shut behind him.

Lila stood in the silence.

Then she heard it—the growl of an engine, tires spitting gravel.

She moved to the window.

Tomas’s black sedan tore down the drive, heading toward the coast road. The rear window glinted in the sunlight—just for a second—before it disappeared around the bend.

Lila waited.

A shadow detached from the side of the house.

Diego stepped into view, pausing beneath the skeletal remains of the old oak. He looked up at the mansion, then back at her.

And then he walked away.

Chapter 4

The Holloway Shipping headquarters squatted over Monterey Bay like a ship beached on cement. Its glass facade reflected the pier, the tuna boats, the whole damn waterfront—all of it in perfect, unnatural symmetry.

Conrad Holloway III stood at the podium in the executive suite, his navy suit cutting a straight line from shoulder to cuff. The room smelled of leather and coffee, the kind of scent that cost more than most houses. Microphones sprouted from the table like a bed of sea urchins.

“Ladies and gentlemen,” Conrad said, his voice smooth as diesel in calm water. “Today we celebrate not just restoration, but renewal. El Estero isn’t just a wetland. It’s proof that Monterey’s future isn’t written in red ink or abandoned docks. It’s written in green.”

A ripple of polite applause. Cameras flashed like fireflies trapped in glass.

Beside him, the Holloway CFO shifted his weight. A bead of sweat tracked down his temple.

Conrad didn’t notice. “This project would not have been possible without the vision of my father, the generosity of the Holloway Foundation, and the tireless work of our ecological partners.” He paused. “And of course, the silent partners who believe in this city’s rebirth.”

A reporter from the Monterey Herald raised a hand. “Can you name the major donors involved in the El Estero project?”

Conrad’s smile didn’t waver. “We respect their privacy. Philanthropy isn’t about plaques or press. It’s about impact.”

The CFO cleared his throat. Conrad ignored him.

Diego Rivas leaned against the back wall, arms crossed, his faded UC Santa Cruz tee swallowed by the jacket he’d stolen from his father’s closet. He watched the livestream on a cracked tablet, the feed buffered by the building’s firewall. The screen flickered—static, then Conrad’s smug face again.

He zoomed in on the podium. Conrad’s cufflinks glinted under the studio lights. *Solid gold. Holloway Shipping engraved.*

Diego’s jaw tightened.

He pulled up the El Estero funding report from the city archives. The numbers scrolled slow, tedious. State grants. Private trusts. A line item—$2.3 million from “Voss Silent Partners Inc.”

He tapped the screen. The name pulsed like a bruise.

Lila’s last name. Her family’s name.

After everything. After years of silence. After her walking out on Conrad in front of the whole damn city. She was still funding the wetlands. Still sneaking money back into the places Holloway had bled dry.

Diego’s thumb hovered over the screen. He could screenshot it. Send it to Lila. *Proof.*

Or he could watch Conrad take the credit.

He chose the latter.

Conrad finished his speech to polite applause. Reporters jostled, voices overlapping, questions firing like machine gun bursts.

Diego slipped out the back stairwell, the tablet tucked under his arm. The fire door slammed behind him. The stairwell smelled of disinfectant and old carpet glue.

His phone buzzed in his pocket. Unknown number.

He answered. “Yeah.”

“Diego.” Tomas Rivas’s voice, smooth as motor oil. “Where are you?”

“None of your business.” Diego took the stairs two at a time. “What do you want?”

“Conrad just gave a press conference.” Tomas’s breath hitched, ever so slightly. “He took credit for El Estero.”

Diego paused on the landing. “Shocking.”

“He also mentioned a ‘withdrawal of key support’ from the Harbor redevelopment vote.” Tomas’s words were measured, precise. “Something about a bureaucratic glitch.”

Diego’s pulse jumped. “What kind of glitch?”

“A major donor pulled their backing.” Tomas’s voice dropped. “The one who was supposed to speak at tomorrow’s chamber meeting.”

Diego’s grip tightened on the railing. “Who was it?”

“Anonymous. Standard procedure.” Tomas exhaled. “But the timing—after your little visit to the Voss mansion—”

“It wasn’t me.”

“Wasn’t it?” Tomas’s tone sharpened. “Because Conrad’s attorney just flagged an unusual transaction from the Voss estate’s accounts. Large sum. Redirected to a shell entity.”

Diego’s mouth went dry. “You’re saying Lila did this?”

“Or someone acting in her name.” Tomas paused. “Diego, listen to me. Conrad is not a man who takes kindly to being undermined. If this escalates—”

“Then he’ll have to deal with it.” Diego pushed through the final door into the employee parking lot. Sunlight hit his face, harsh and sudden. “I’m not your messenger boy anymore, Tomas.”

Silence. Then, the faintest click—Tomas ending the call.

Diego tossed the phone onto the passenger seat of his battered Jeep. He started the engine. The stereo crackled—old Rage Against the Machine, turned down low.

He pulled out of the lot, tires spitting gravel.

Conrad Holloway III would not get to rewrite the story.

Not again.

Chapter 5

The Holloway Shipping boardroom smelled of aged mahogany and the sharp citrus of an overworked air freshener. Conrad Holloway III stood at the head of the table, his tailored suit jacket draped over the back of his chair, sleeves rolled to the elbow. His cufflinks—solid gold, Holloway Shipping engraved—glinted under the recessed lighting.

Tomas Rivas sat across from him, fingers steepled, his Rolex catching the light every time he shifted his wrist. The accountant, a man with the posture of a man who’d spent too many years staring at spreadsheets, hovered near the credenza, a tablet balanced on one palm.

Conrad didn’t look up from the papers in front of him. “Tomas, I’ll need the full ledger by close of business tomorrow. Every line item reviewed for accuracy.”

Tomas exhaled through his nose. “Conrad, these are local charities we’re talking about. The kind of organizations that thrive on discretion.”

“Discretion is a luxury I’m no longer afforded.” Conrad tapped a pen against the table. The sound echoed like a metronome counting down. “Especially when those same charities suddenly decide to flout city ordinances.”

The accountant’s Adam’s apple bobbed. He cleared his throat. “The Monterey Bay Conservancy filed a variance request yesterday. They’re citing ‘ecological urgency.’”

Conrad’s pen stilled. “And their largest donor?”

The accountant glanced at Tomas, who didn’t meet his eyes.

Conrad set the pen down. “Which is?”

Tomas finally looked up. “Anonymous.”

A muscle in Conrad’s jaw twitched. “Every anonymous donor in this city has a name. Find it.”

The accountant’s fingers flew across the tablet screen. “There’s a payment history. All routed through a shell company—Voss Silent Partners, Inc.”

Conrad’s expression didn’t change, but his fingers curled into fists on the table. “Lila Voss.”

Tomas didn’t answer right away. He smoothed a crease from his dress shirt, his movements deliberate, almost lazy. “Her accounts have been frozen. The executors of her mother’s estate are citing ‘irregularities.’ But there’s something else.”

Conrad leaned forward. “Go on.”

“Recent transactions. Large sums. All redirected.” Tomas tapped the tablet, sliding it across the table toward Conrad. “From Voss Silent Partners to local initiatives. The kind Conrad Holloway III would ordinarily fund himself.”

Conrad didn’t touch the tablet. “And you’re telling me this how?”

Tomas’s smile was thin. “Because Tomas Rivas is nothing if not thorough. Also, because I signed the checks.”

The accountant’s breath hitched. Conrad’s gaze flicked to him, then back to Tomas. The room seemed to shrink.

Conrad’s voice was low, measured. “You signed checks from a company you don’t control?”

Tomas picked up the pen Conrad had set down and twirled it between his fingers. “I signed checks from a company that was supposed to be yours, Conrad. But the ledgers are missing. Financial auditors are asking questions. And the Harbor redevelopment vote is in six weeks.”

Conrad’s chair scraped back as he stood. “Where’s the money?”

Tomas met his gaze. “Gone.”

Conrad’s fist connected with the table. The pen snapped in Tomas’s hand. “You stole from me.”

“I redirected funds that were already stolen from Lila.” Tomas’s voice was steady, almost quiet. “She inherited nothing but debt and a name. You know that better than anyone.”

Conrad’s face darkened. “This isn’t about justice. This is about leverage.”

Tomas wiped the broken pen pieces onto the tablecloth. “And you’re running out of time. The conservancy’s variance hearing is tomorrow. If they get approval, your entire Harbor project gets bogged down in red tape for years.”

Conrad’s lips curled. “You’re positioning yourself as the hero? After years of taking my cut?”

Tomas stood, the chair rolling back with a metallic scrape. “I’m positioning myself as the only one who can keep this city from burning. Including you.”

The accountant took a step back, his tablet slipping from his grasp. It hit the floor with a crack, the screen fracturing into a spiderweb of light.

Conrad stared at the broken screen. The reflection of the overhead lights warped across the shattered glass, a fractured constellation of power.

Then his phone buzzed. He pulled it from his pocket, read the screen, and his expression smoothed into something like satisfaction.

Tomas noticed the shift immediately. “Conrad.”

Conrad slid the phone back into his jacket. “Change of plans. We don’t need the ledger anymore.”

Tomas’s eyes narrowed. “Why?”

Conrad’s smile was slow, knowing. “Because Lila Voss just made her first mistake.”

He turned toward the door, pausing only to collect his suit jacket. “Tomas, clean this up. And find me the records for every shell company Voss Silent Partners has ever touched. Every one.”

The door clicked shut behind him.

Tomas stood alone in the boardroom, the broken tablet at his feet, the scent of shattered pen and old mahogany thick in the air. He looked at the accountant, who was already scrambling to gather the fallen device.

“Voss Silent Partners,” Tomas muttered. “Majority shareholder: Lila Voss Cortez.”

The accountant’s head snapped up. “I’ll pull the corporate filings.”

Tomas didn’t move. He stared at the door Conrad had just walked through, his reflection warped in the polished tabletop.

“Majority shareholder,” he repeated.

Then he picked up his phone.

Chapter 6

The Monterey Bay Lodge shimmered under the late afternoon sun, its white clapboard facade glowing against the deep blue of the bay. Lila stood on the veranda, a glass of sparkling water in hand, watching the caterers adjust the last of the place settings. The wedding was supposed to happen here tomorrow. Instead, the Lodge’s ballroom was being converted into a conservation fundraiser—one that would hand Conrad Holloway a public relations disaster on a silver platter.

She adjusted the silk scarf at her throat, the same shade of seafoam green as the estuary beyond the windows. The scarf was a small rebellion, a splash of color in an otherwise muted palette. The Lodge’s décor was all ivory and gold, the kind of sterile elegance Conrad insisted on. Lila preferred the wild edges, the places where salt ate at wood and wind tore at fabric.

A city council aide approached, phone in hand. “Ms. Cortez, we’re ready for the deed transfer. The title office just confirmed.”

Lila set her glass on the railing. “Already filed?”

The aide nodded. “Electronically. The land’s in El Estero’s name now.”

She didn’t smile. “Good.”

Conrad’s lawyer appeared at the top of the steps, his phone already ringing before he reached the veranda. His name was Giles Wainwright—sharp suit, sharper jaw, and the kind of smile that never quite reached his eyes. He stopped two paces away, as if proximity to her might contaminate him.

“Lila,” he said, voice smooth as oil. “Conrad Holloway III’s office. They’d like a word about the waterfront property transfer.”

Lila picked up her glass again, took a slow sip. “Which one?”

Wainwright’s jaw tightened. “The one you just donated to Monterey Bay Conservancy.”

She set the glass down. “I didn’t donate anything. An anonymous patron did.”

“Anonymous?” Wainwright’s laugh was a dry click. “There’s no such thing in this town.”

“Strange,” she said. “I seem to recall Conrad Holloway III built an empire on anonymity.”

Wainwright stepped closer, lowering his voice. “You know as well as I do that the land was under Holloway control. The variance hearings were scheduled for next week.”

“Funny,” Lila said. “The conservancy filed theirs this morning. Approved before lunch.”

Wainwright’s fingers twitched around his phone. “This isn’t over.”

She turned to look at the bay, where a pelican rode the wind just above the water’s surface. “It was over the moment Conrad decided to steal from children’s hospitals and wetland restorations.”

A flicker of something like anger crossed Wainwright’s face. Then it was gone, replaced by the practiced neutrality of a man who spent his life in boardrooms and backrooms. “You’re playing a dangerous game.”

“No,” Lila said. “I’m playing the only game left.”

Wainwright lifted his phone, tapped the screen. “Conrad wants the land back. We’ll pay market rate.”

She laughed, a single sharp sound that made a server pause mid-step. “Conrad doesn’t own market rate. He owns what he steals. And El Estero doesn’t sell.”

Wainwright’s eyes narrowed. “You’re slipping up, Lila. Using your own name in the transfer? After all this time?”

She met his gaze. “I’m not using my name. Voss Silent Partners is.”

He hesitated. The name hung between them, unspoken until now.

“Voss Silent Partners is a shell,” Wainwright said. “And shells have owners.”

“Then ask the owner,” she said. “But you won’t like the answer.”

Wainwright’s phone buzzed. He glanced at the screen, then back at her. “You just made a mistake.”

She picked up her glass again. “Already have.”

He turned on his heel and walked away, phone already pressed to his ear. Lila watched him go, then refocused on the bay. The pelican had landed on the piling now, wings folded, eyeing the water for fish.

Diego stepped onto the veranda from the side door, hands in his pockets, the sleeves of his faded chambray shirt rolled to the elbows. He hadn’t shaved in two days, the dark stubble catching the light. A bruise lingered under his left eye, yellowing at the edges.

Lila didn’t look at him. “You’re late.”

“Traffic from Santa Cruz.” He stopped beside her, close enough that she caught the scent of salt and sunscreen. “Heard the news about the land.”

She exhaled through her nose. “Conrad’s threatening legal action.”

“He’ll lose.” Diego’s voice was quiet. “The deed’s already filed. No judge in Monterey’s going to overturn it.”

She finally looked at him. “You sound sure.”

He met her gaze, dark eyes steady. “Because I am.”

A gust of wind lifted the scarf from her throat, sending it fluttering toward the bay. Diego caught it before it could escape, his fingers brushing the silk. She froze.

He held the scarf out to her. “You’re welcome.”

She took it, their fingers touching for just a second longer than necessary. Heat flared where his skin met hers, then faded as quickly as it came.

“Why are you here, Diego?”

“Same reason you are.” He gestured toward the Lodge’s lawn, where conservationists were unloading equipment for the fundraiser. “El Estero’s not just your fight anymore.”

She wrapped the scarf around her throat again, tighter than before. “It never was.”

“Bullshit.” His voice was low, rough. “You funded it alone for years. But you’re not alone anymore.”

She turned to face him fully. “That’s not what this is.”

“Then what is it?”

The question hung between them, heavy with years of silence and the unspoken weight of everything they’d lost.

She looked away first, back at the bay. The pelican had taken flight again, wings beating hard against the wind.

“It’s a ledger,” she said. “And it’s the only thing Conrad wants more than my silence.”

Diego’s jaw tightened. “The one from your mother’s piano.”

She didn’t answer.

He stepped closer, close enough that she felt the heat of him through the silk of her blouse. “You burned the others. But you kept that one.”

“Because it’s not just mine.” Her voice was barely above a whisper. “It’s yours.”

He stilled. “What?”

She reached into her pocket, pulled out a folded sheet of paper. The edges were singed, the ink smudged in places from heat. She held it out, but didn’t let go when he reached for it.

“Your father’s embezzlement,” she said. “The one that started the chain reaction. Your mother’s suicide wasn’t just grief. It was ruin.”

Diego’s fingers closed around the paper, but his hand didn’t move. “Where did you get this?”

“From your journals.” She met his gaze. “The ones you hid in the boathouse.”

His breath came faster. “You went through my things?”

“Yes.”

“Why?”

“Because I needed to know.” She pulled her hand back. “And because you’re the only one who can help me recover the last ledger—the one that proves Conrad Holloway’s entire empire is built on stolen time, stolen money, stolen lives.”

Diego stared at the paper in his hand, the smudged ink under the lamplight. His knuckles were white.

Then his phone buzzed. He pulled it from his pocket, glanced at the screen. His face went still.

Lila knew before he spoke. Conrad had already moved.

“Diego?” she asked.

He looked at her, eyes dark with something she couldn’t name. “They’re calling it over.”

“What?”

“The Harbor redevelopment vote.” He slid the phone back into his pocket. “Moved up. Three days from now.”

She felt the ground tilt under her feet. “Conrad did this.”

Diego nodded. “He’s not waiting for the ledger anymore. He’s going for the jugular.”

She wrapped her arms around herself, the scarf forgotten. The wind picked up, tugging at her sleeves, her hair, the edges of her composure.

Diego stepped closer again, close enough that she felt the heat of him against the chill creeping up her spine. “We can still stop him,” he said. “But we have to move now.”

She looked up at him. “We?”

His hand found the small of her back, not quite a touch, not quite an embrace. Just enough to ground her.

“Yeah,” he said. “We.”

Chapter 7

The grand hall of the Monterey Bay Lodge smelled of candle wax and chilled champagne. Lila adjusted the emerald cuffs of her dress, the only color in a room draped in white and silver. The charity gala for the Monterey Arts Foundation had started twenty minutes ago. She had arrived late on purpose.

Clayton Reeves stood near the auction podium, his tuxedo too crisp, his smile too practiced. He was laughing at something the mayor’s wife had said, but his eyes flicked to Lila the moment she entered. The laughter died. His fingers tightened around his flute of champagne.

She didn’t look away.

Conrad Holloway III stood near the fireplace, a glass of bourbon in hand, surrounded by a circle of men in tailored suits. They spoke in low, urgent tones, but Lila caught fragments—“unexplained funding gaps,” “quarterly shortfalls,” “audit recommendations.” Her lips twitched. Audit recommendations. As if anyone in this room could audit their way out of the Holloway fortune’s rot.

A server passed with a tray of stuffed mushrooms. Lila took one, popped it into her mouth. The filling was too rich. She set the tray down untouched.

Clayton excused himself from the mayor’s wife and crossed the room. His smile was a knife with the edge filed off.

“Lila,” he said. “You look… well.”

She lifted her glass of sparkling water in a silent toast. “Clayton.”

He leaned in, voice low. “The wedding’s off. You know that.”

“Mmm.” She took a sip. “I’d heard.”

“People are talking.”

“Let them.”

His jaw flexed. “You humiliate me in front of the entire city, then act like it’s nothing.”

She set her glass down. “I didn’t humiliate you, Clayton. You did that when you decided to tell the press I was ‘too emotionally unstable’ to run the Voss Foundation.”

“It wasn’t personal.”

“It was always personal.” She stepped closer, just enough that he had to tilt his head down to meet her gaze. “You wanted a bride, not a partner. And certainly not one who signs checks with seven figures.”

Clayton’s fingers twitched. “You’re playing with fire.”

“Am I?” She smiled, slow and cool. “Funny. I thought I was just lighting the fuse.”

He laughed, sharp and humorless. “You think you’re Conrad Holloway? Playing puppet master from behind some shell company?”

“Not a shell,” she said. “A mirror.”

Clayton’s eyes darkened. “You’re going to lose.”

“Not as spectacularly as you.” She turned away. “Good luck with the rest of your life, Clayton. Though I suspect it’ll feel like a very long consolation prize.”

He reached for her wrist. She sidestepped, fluid as water around a rock.

Conrad Holloway III’s voice cut across the room like a blade. “Clayton. A word.”

Clayton’s grip loosened. He didn’t look at Conrad. Didn’t dare.

Lila adjusted her scarf, ironically the same shade of seafoam green as the bay beyond the windows. Conrad’s gaze locked onto it. His jaw tightened.

She lifted her glass in another silent toast. His eyes narrowed.

Giles Wainwright appeared at Conrad’s side, murmuring something in his ear. Conrad’s bourbon sloshed as he set the glass down. He said something to the group, then walked toward Lila.

She didn’t flinch.

Conrad stopped three feet away, close enough that she smelled the leather of his shoes and the faint metallic tang of aftershave. “You’re playing a dangerous game, Lila.”

She tilted her head. “You keep saying that. I’m not the one who embezzled from children’s cancer funds.”

His face didn’t change. “You don’t know what you’re talking about.”

“Then explain to me,” she said, “why the Monterey Bay Conservancy received an unprecedented grant yesterday. One that covers their operating costs for the next two years. Funny thing—no donor listed.”

Conrad’s fingers curled into a loose fist. “Coincidence.”

“Or karmic accounting.” She took a slow sip of water. “Funny how the Holloway shell companies have been ‘overfunding’ the conservancy for years. And yet, suddenly, there’s a shortfall.”

Wainwright stepped forward. “Ms. Cortez. This is private—”

Conrad cut him off with a raised hand. “You think you’ve outmaneuvered me.”

“I know I have.”

Conrad leaned in, voice a rasp. “You signed those transfer papers today. The land’s already gone. But the ledger? That’s still mine.”

She met his gaze. “Your name isn’t on it.”

“It will be.” His breath was hot against her ear. “One way or another.”

She stepped back. “Careful, Conrad. You’re starting to sound desperate.”

His face darkened. For a second, she saw the man beneath the veneer—the one who had built an empire on theft and lies. Then the mask slid back into place.

“Enjoy the gala, Lila,” he said. “It’s your last.”

She watched him walk away, his back rigid, his posture broadcasting control. But she saw the slight hesitation in his step, the way his fingers brushed his thigh, searching for a phone that wasn’t vibrating.

Wainwright lingered, his eyes darting between her and Conrad. “You’re making a mistake.”

She smiled. “No. You are.”

He turned and followed Conrad into the hallway.

Lila exhaled, slow and controlled. The room seemed too warm. She stepped onto the terrace, the cool night air a slap to her senses. The Lodge’s veranda overlooked the bay, the water black and restless under the moonlight.

A voice came from the shadows. “You’re bleeding.”

Diego Rivas stepped into the light, his shirt unbuttoned at the collar, sleeves rolled to the elbows. A cut glistened on his jawline, fresh and dark.

She didn’t ask how he got it. “You’re trespassing.”

“Party’s open to the public. Technically.” He stopped beside her, close enough that she felt the heat of him against the chill in the air. “You look like you need an escape route.”

She leaned against the railing. “I was just leaving.”

“Funny. You haven’t touched a single thing.” He nodded toward her untouched glass on the table inside. “Not even the champagne.”

She followed his gaze. “Clayton’s poisoned well is dry.”

Diego laughed, low and rough. “You always did have a flair for the dramatic.”

She turned to face him. “Why are you here, Diego?”

He looked out at the bay, where a fishing boat’s wake cut through the water, a silver thread dissolving into black. “Because you lit a fire under Conrad, and now he’s running with gasoline.”

“And?”

“And,” he said, “when Conrad runs with gasoline, someone gets burned.”

She crossed her arms. “You could’ve called.”

“You wouldn’t have answered.” He met her gaze. “You only talk when you’re cornered.”

She exhaled through her nose. “What do you want?”

“Your help.” He pulled a folded paper from his pocket, held it out. “Before Tomas burns it.”

She didn’t take it. “My father’s old law partner? What does he have to do with anything?”

“He’s been skimming from your silent donations.” Diego unfolded the paper. A ledger page, edges charred, ink smudged. “Tomas redirected funds meant for the homeless shelter to his private accounts. Then he tried to cover it by redirecting more from the conservancy.”

She looked at the paper. The numbers were familiar. Too familiar.

“You kept this?”

“I kept everything.” He folded it back up. “Including the fact that Voss Silent Partners doesn’t just fund ventures. It owns them.”

Her stomach twisted. “Conrad doesn’t know that.”

“He will.” Diego’s voice was low, urgent. “And when he does, he’ll come for you.”

She finally took the paper. Her fingers brushed his. The contact was brief, but the heat lingered.

“Why give this to me now?”

“Because your wedding was canceled. The timeline just accelerated.” He stepped closer, close enough that she felt the scrape of his sleeve against her bare arm. “And because I’m done watching you play hero alone.”

She looked at the paper, the smudged ink, the familiar columns of numbers. Then at Diego—his dark eyes, the bruise under his cheekbone, the way his breath came just a little too fast.

“You’re blackmailing me,” she said.

He almost smiled. “I’m offering you a partner.”

She tucked the paper into her clutch. “I don’t need a partner.”

“Yes,” he said. “You do.”

She turned back to the bay. The fishing boat had vanished. The water was still, reflecting nothing.

“I’ll think about it,” she said.

Diego didn’t move. “You have until Conrad’s next move. Which could be tonight.”

She looked at him. “And if I say no?”

He held her gaze. “Then you lose.”

The terrace door opened. A waiter stepped out, tray in hand. “Ms. Cortez, your car is here.”

She nodded. “Thank you.”

Diego stepped back. “Goodbye, Lila.”

She didn’t answer. She walked past him, her heels clicking against the wooden deck. The waiter held the door. She stepped inside, the warmth of the gala swallowing her whole.

The door swung shut behind her.

Conrad Holloway III’s phone buzzed on the table beside his untouched bourbon. He picked it up, read the alert. His face went still.

*Local NGO Receives Unprecedented Grant—Donor Unknown.*

Chapter 8

Conrad Holloway III’s office smelled of aged leather, aged whiskey, and the faint metallic tang of sea salt that seeped through the Monterey Harbor’s salt-laden winds. The city outside was quiet at this hour, the kind of quiet that came after a gala—a quiet that hid the fires burning beneath the surface.

Tomas Rivas stood by the fireplace, feeding a manila folder into the flames. The paper curled, blackened at the edges, then burst into embers. He used the poker to press the ashes flat, watching as they crumbled into dust.

Diego Rivas stepped through the unlocked door without knocking. The office lights were off. He moved through the dark, his boots silent on the Persian rug. He saw the bottle of bourbon on the desk—half-empty, expensive, untouched.

Tomas didn’t turn. “This isn’t a social call.”

Diego stopped three feet from the desk. “You’re burning evidence.”

Tomas exhaled through his nose. “Evidence of what?”

“Evidence of embezzlement. Again.” Diego’s voice was level, but his hands flexed at his sides. “You took from the conservancy. You took from the homeless shelter. You took from Lila’s private accounts.”

Tomas picked up the bourbon, swirled it, then set it down. “Lila’s accounts are her family’s legacy. She doesn’t need every penny tracked like a charity case.”

“She doesn’t need them stolen either.”

Tomas finally turned. His face was gaunt, his eyes bloodshot. “You know nothing about legacy.”

“We both know you’ve been siphoning off her silent donations for years. Skimming the top, calling it ‘administrative fees.’” Diego stepped closer. “She trusted you.”

“She trusted the wrong people.” Tomas’s voice was icy. “Her mother did. Her father did. And look where that got them.”

Diego flinched. The name wasn’t spoken aloud in polite company, but it hung between them like a ghost.

Tomas saw the reaction. A slow, humorless smile spread across his face. “Oh, you still haven’t told her, have you?”

Diego didn’t answer.

Tomas picked up a ledger from the desk. A single page was folded outward. He held it over the flames. “You know what this is?”

Diego didn’t move. “I know what you’re trying to burn.”

Tomas unfolded it slightly. The page was clean, typed in a precise font. Lines of numbers, dates, shell company names.

Lila Voss Cortez—at the top.

The ink was fresh. Unburnable.

Diego’s stomach twisted. He knew what this meant. Knew what it would do to her.

Tomas saw his expression. “She’s not who you think she is.”

Diego didn’t look away. “And you’re not who she thinks you are.”

Tomas laughed. “You two were always too alike. Stubborn. Proud. Convinced the world owed you something.”

Diego reached for the ledger. “Give it to me.”

“Why?” Tomas pulled it back. “You’ll take it to her? Let her see how much you’ve underestimated her?”

“She deserves to know the truth.”

“Truth?” Tomas scoffed. “You think truth matters here? Conrad’s got the Harbor vote locked. The city’s about to hand him a blank check. And Lila? She’s already lost.”

Diego lunged.

Tomas dodged, but Diego caught his wrist. The ledger slipped from Tomas’s fingers, fluttering to the floor. Diego snatched it up—

Tomas kicked the poker. It clattered against the fireplace, sending a shower of sparks into the air.

Diego didn’t flinch. He had the ledger.

Tomas’s face twisted. “You don’t understand what you’re holding.”

“I understand enough.” Diego stepped back, tucking the ledger into his jacket. “You’ve been playing both sides. Lila and Conrad. Stealing from her, feeding him intel. You’re the reason El Estero’s funding collapsed.”

Tomas’s breath came fast. “You think that matters now? Conrad’s audits are coming. His routes are frozen. The Harbor deal is dead. And when it dies, so does she.”

Diego’s grip tightened on the ledger. “You’re scared.”

“Of what?” Tomas’s voice cracked. “Losing my cut? My leverage? My damn life? Conrad’s not the only one who knows how to make people disappear.”

Diego didn’t answer. He turned toward the door.

Tomas’s voice followed him. “You take that to her, Diego, and you seal your fate. She’ll see you for what you are—a scavenger. Just like her.”

Diego paused at the door. “I know exactly what I am.”

He stepped into the hallway. The office door clicked shut behind him.

The floorboards creaked under his weight as he walked. He didn’t look back.

The ledger was heavy in his jacket. He could feel the weight of it, the weight of what it meant.

He rounded the corner, heading for the stairwell, when his phone buzzed in his pocket. He pulled it out.

Unknown number.

The message was short.

*You took something that doesn’t belong to you.*

Diego’s jaw tightened.

He typed back. *It belongs to Lila.*

The reply came instantly.

*Not anymore.*

The screen went dark.

Diego stared at it for a long moment. Then he pocketed the phone.

He took the stairs two at a time, descending into the dim glow of the harbor’s night lights.

The ledger in his jacket felt like a bomb.

And he was about to deliver it straight to the fuse.

Chapter 9

The Cannery Row Café sat in the bend of the old sardine cannery, its patio crowded with tourists clutching chowder bowls and selfie sticks. Inside, the air was cool and smelled of coffee and warm croissants. Conrad Holloway III sat alone at a corner table, a silver thermos beside him, untouched.

Lila paused at the entrance. The maître d’ nodded her forward. Conrad saw her and rose, one hand lifting—calling her to him like a dog.

She walked over. The heels of her sandals tapped against the tile.

Conrad didn’t smile. “You’re late.”

Lila sat. She opened her menu, glanced at the prices, then set it down. “You’re welcome.”

The server appeared. She ordered Earl Grey. Conrad didn’t look up from his thermos. “I was beginning to think you’d avoid me forever.”

“After your little stunt at the lodge? I’ve been busy.”

“I meant what I said.” He uncapped the thermos, poured dark liquid into a paper cup. “I know you think you’re playing a game. But this isn’t chess, Lila. This is life.”

She picked up the sugar spoon, turned it in her fingers. “I know exactly what this is.”

Conrad’s jaw tightened. “Then you know I can end you with a phone call.”

“You could try.” She looked past him, toward the harbor. The water glittered. “But you won’t.”

He leaned forward. “I’m offering you a partnership. Your money, my influence. Half the Harbor redevelopment under your name. Full control over the conservation grants. You’d finally have what you’ve been pretending to want—real power, real legacy.”

Lila sipped her tea. The steam curled between them. “I already have influence.”

“Not like this.” He pushed a manila folder across the table. It landed beside her teacup. “Here’s the breakdown. Your silent donations? Public now. Tomas’s embezzlement? Buried. Clayton’s indiscretions? Handled. You walk away clean. I walk away solvent. Everyone wins.”

She didn’t touch the folder. “And what do you get?”

“A free hand. No more sneaking around. No more charity masquerades.” He tapped the folder. “You’re tired of being the saint. Admit it.”

Lila folded her hands. “You’re offering me your shame in exchange for my silence.”

Conrad’s laugh was sharp. “It’s not shame when it’s legal. It’s business.”

She picked up the folder, flipped it open. The first page listed shell companies, dates, amounts. Holloway Shipping. Monterey Bay Lodge. Tomas Rivas Law. Each line had a red checkmark.

Lila closed the folder. “You don’t get it, Conrad.”

“Then explain it to me.”

A shadow fell across the table.

Diego Rivas stood there, sleeves rolled to his elbows, a manila folder in his hand. His hair was damp, as if he’d walked from the water. He dropped the folder beside hers. The teacups rattled.

Conrad’s face darkened. “What’s this?”

Diego didn’t look at him. “Just what you asked for, Lila.”

She opened his folder. Inside was a single sheet of paper, typed in a clean font. At the top: *Holloway & Rivas Maritime Consolidated—Memorandum of Understanding.*

Her eyes flicked down. Crude handwriting in the margin: *Transfer of Shares—Diego Rivas—20% controlling interest—'

Conrad stood so fast his chair screeched. He snatched the paper from her hands.

Diego didn’t move. “That was going to the IRS next week. Thought you might want to see it first.”

Conrad’s face went gray. “You—you little worm—”

Diego finally looked at him. “I’m not the one who’s been lying for twenty years.”

Conrad crumpled the paper in his fist. He threw the wad at Diego’s chest. It bounced off, fell to the floor. Diego didn’t flinch.

The patio went quiet. Tourists turned, phones raised.

Conrad’s voice was a growl. “This changes nothing.”

Lila stood. The chair slid back with a scrape. She picked up her purse. “It changes everything.”

Conrad’s eyes locked onto hers. “You think you’re untouchable?”

She smiled. It wasn’t kind. “I don’t think. I know.”

Conrad stormed past Diego, shoulder knocking him hard enough to make him stumble. Diego caught the edge of the table, steadied himself. Conrad didn’t look back as he disappeared into the crowd.

The server brought the check. Lila signed it without glancing at the amount.

Diego picked up the crumpled paper from the floor. He smoothed it out on the table. “He’s going to burn this town down.”

Lila tucked her napkin beside her plate. “Not if we burn his ledgers first.”

Diego looked at her. Really looked at her. The years between them felt like a door ajar—just enough light to see the shape of what was lost.

She met his gaze. “Walk me to the car.”

He didn’t ask why. He just nodded.

They stepped into the bright afternoon sun. The harbor breeze carried the scent of salt and diesel. Lila’s black Mercedes idled at the curb.

Diego opened the passenger door for her.

She paused before getting in. “You’re still a scavenger, Diego.”

He closed the door for her. “And you’re still the only one who didn’t need saving.”

The car’s engine purred to life. Diego stepped back as she pulled away, tires crunching on the gravel.

He watched the taillights disappear around the corner.

Then he opened his folder again.

Inside was a blueprint. Monterey Harbor. Red lines. Blue lines. A single word stamped in the margin: *Executive.*

His phone buzzed. Unknown number.

He answered. “This is Diego.”

A voice, rough and familiar, filled his ear. “You took something that belongs to Conrad. You took something that belongs to me.”

Diego’s grip tightened. “Who is this?”

The line went dead.

He stood there, on the cracked pavement of Cannery Row, the folder heavy in his hands, the harbor’s noise suddenly too loud in his ears.

The blueprint smelled like ink and old paper and the weight of a deal signed in blood.

He knew where this led.

Conrad would come for him. Not with lawyers. With muscle.

And tonight, he had a date with a ledger that could sink them both.

Chapter 10

The abandoned Voss mansion stood crooked on its foundations, salt wind eating the cedar shingles. Lila parked behind a rusted fishing trawler’s hull propped on cinder blocks. The net draped over the truck bed fluttered like a ghost in the evening breeze.

Diego’s motorcycle was already there, leaning against the sagging porch. Its chrome caught the last light. He didn’t wave. Didn’t need to.

She stepped over broken glass and climbed the sagging stairs. The front door hung open on one hinge, sighing in the wind.

Inside, the air smelled of mildew and old varnish. Sunbeams cut through dust motes, painting stripes on the warped floorboards.

“Upstairs,” his voice came from the shadows.

She found him in the attic. The roof had leaked, but the space was dry. Maps covered every surface. Blueprints weighted corners with rocks. A single desk lamp threw a yellow circle onto a wooden table where a red ledger book lay open.

Diego didn’t look up from unfolding a large architectural sheet across the table. The paper crackled like dried leaves.

Lila stepped closer. The blueprint was Monterey Harbor—every dock, warehouse, and slip marked in precise lines. Red pen traced routes between them, circled numbers, names she recognized.

“Holloway & Rivas Maritime,” she said.

Diego tapped a red line connecting the downtown piers. “Your silent stake. Twenty-three percent of all shipping routes in and out of Monterey. Started when you were sixteen.”

She picked up a smaller sheet. “And this?”

“Monterey Bay Lodge renovation. Sixteen million funneled through fake environmental grants.” He traced a finger along a blue line. “You own the land, the permits, the damn bistro.”

Lila flipped another page. Tomas Rivas Law. Holloway Shipping Holdings. Monterey Peninsula Foundation. Each had a single red asterisk beside Lila’s name in the margin.

“Why asterisks?”

Diego finally met her eyes. “Because you don’t just fund them. You own them.” He reached into his jacket and pulled out a plastic sleeve. Inside was a single sheet of paper.

She unfolded it. Title: *Voss Silent Partners—Memorandum of Corporate Control.*

Her breath caught. The document listed five shell companies—Holloway Coastal Holdings, Monterey Bay Development Trust, El Estero Conservation Fund, Peninsula Philanthropic Network, and Rivas Maritime Consolidated. Her name wasn’t mentioned once.

At the bottom, in bold type: *Lila Voss Cortez—Executive Managing Director.*

Diego tapped the last line. “You’re not just a benefactor. You’re the majority shareholder in every venture Conrad Holloway III claims as his empire.”

Lila sat heavily on a wooden crate. The blueprint crinkled beneath her fingers. “All this time...”

“All this time you played the grieving heiress while pulling the strings.” He folded his arms. “Even El Estero. The wetland you saved? You bought the debt from Holloway Shipping in 2020. Own it outright.”

She stared at the asterisk beside El Estero. The land she’d saved from development. The birds she’d brought back.

Diego leaned over the table. “The Harbor redevelopment vote isn’t just delayed—it’s dead unless we let it happen.”

Lila looked up. “What do you mean?”

He tapped a red line connecting the old cannery district to the marina. “Conrad’s project needs your silent shares to pass city council. Without them, the vote fails. The Harbor Commission won’t approve anything until the election next year.”

She traced the line with her fingertip. The ink was wet, as if freshly drawn. “So if I sell my shares...”

“Conrad gets his development. The environmental studies die. The wetlands get drained.” Diego rolled up the blueprint, secured it with an elastic band. “But if we let the vote fail now, Conrad loses face. His financing dries up. His investors start asking questions.”

Lila exhaled slowly. The attic felt suddenly smaller. The weight of the ledger pressed into her palms.

“And what do we do with all this?” she asked.

Diego pulled a small flash drive from his pocket. “We make it public. Every shell company name. Every embezzled dollar. Every backroom deal. In one week, it leaks to every news outlet from here to San Francisco.”

She took the drive, turned it in her fingers. “Then what?”

“Then Conrad Holloway III gets exactly what he deserves.” Diego’s voice was quiet. “Not just his company exposed. His entire empire.”

Lila looked back at the blueprint. The red lines looked like wounds.

“And us?” she asked. “What happens to us after?”

Diego hesitated. He picked up a rusted nail from the table, turned it in his fingers. “You’ll be a fraud. A liar. Conrad will make sure of that. Your wedding—”

“Is already ruined,” she finished.

He met her gaze. “Is that what you want?”

The question hung between them. The attic creaked in the wind.

Lila reached for the ledger. She flipped to a page near the back. The handwriting wasn’t hers. It was older. Her mother’s.

*For Lila—when you’re ready.*

Diego stepped closer. He didn’t touch her. Didn’t need to.

She closed the ledger. “We need to move fast. Conrad will come after the drive first.”

Diego nodded. He pulled out his phone, tapped a number. “I’ll get a safe house. Somewhere he can’t trace.”

The call connected. He didn’t wait for a greeting. “It’s me. We need out. Now.”

Lila picked up a framed photo from the desk. It was her mother, younger, smiling on a boat. The same boat Lila had burned in Chapter 3.

Diego hung up. “They’ll pick us up at the old Coast Guard station in twenty. We go in separate cars. No phones. No trails.”

She set the photo down carefully. The glass was cracked. “He’ll know it was us.”

“Conrad always thinks he’s three steps ahead.” Diego tucked the flash drive into his front pocket, next to his wallet. “Tonight, he’ll find out he’s three steps behind.”

Lila’s fingers brushed the ledger. The paper was thin, fragile. Everything she’d built stood in this fragile book.

Diego checked his watch. “We need to go.”

She nodded. But she didn’t move.

He turned back, waited.

The attic was silent except for the wind. Somewhere below, a floorboard groaned.

Lila finally stood. She tucked the ledger into her bag, slung it over her shoulder.

Diego held out his hand. Not for her. For the ledger.

She hesitated, then gave it to him. He slipped it into a padded envelope from his jacket. Watched her face as he sealed it.

“Last chance,” he said. “Walk away. Let Conrad burn. Let the Harbor get drained. It doesn’t have to be you.”

She stepped closer. Her sandal caught on a floorboard. She steadied herself against the table.

“It does,” she said. “It always has.”

Diego didn’t answer. He tucked the envelope securely inside his jacket. Then he took her hand.

The attic door slammed shut downstairs.

They both froze.

Diego’s grip tightened. His eyes locked onto the stairs.

Lila didn’t turn around. She knew. Conrad’s men.

The attic windows rattled. Distant voices, heavy footsteps on the rotting floor.

Diego pulled her toward the back window. Pushed it open. Salt air rushed in.

“Go,” he whispered.

Lila climbed onto the sill. Below, the cracked backyard stretched toward the dunes. The ocean roared beyond.

Diego’s phone buzzed. He glanced at it. His face went pale.

“Conrad’s already at the Coast Guard station,” he said. “They’re waiting.”

The attic door burst open upstairs.

Diego grabbed her wrist. “Run.”

She jumped. The ground rushed up to meet her. Sand sprayed beneath her feet as she sprinted toward the dunes. The wind stole her breath. Salt stung her eyes.

Behind her, Diego’s motorcycle roared to life. Headlights cut through the twilight.

She didn’t look back. Didn’t need to.

She knew he was coming.

Her phone buzzed in her pocket. Unknown number.

She didn’t answer. Kept running.

The dunes swallowed her silhouette. The ocean swallowed her footsteps.

Somewhere behind her, a gunshot cracked. Not aimed at her. Aimed at Diego.

She didn’t flinch. Didn’t slow.

Her breath came fast. Her bag bounced against her hip. The ledger was safe.

And Conrad Holloway III just realized he’d been playing chess with a queen who moved in silence.

Her feet hit pavement. A car door opened ahead.

She dove inside. The engine growled. Tires spat gravel.

Through the window, she saw Diego’s motorcycle fishtail onto the road, headlight weaving wildly.

The car accelerated. The dunes vanished in the rearview.

Lila finally looked at her phone.

A single text glowed on the screen:

*Time to choose your side, Lila. Mine or his.*

Chapter 11

The Monterey Bay Lodge bar smelled of lemon cleaner and aged wood. Lila swirled her champagne, watching ice cubes clink against the flute’s rim. The bartender polished the same stemmed glass for the third time in five minutes. Outside the floor-to-ceiling windows, Monterey Bay stretched dark and restless under a bruised sky.

Her phone lit up. A push alert from the *Monterey Herald*: “Tomas Rivas, Monterey’s Beloved Attorney, Arrested on Embezzlement Charges.”

She didn’t reach for it.

Across the room, a group of wedding planners clustered around a mood board, jabbering about floral installations. One woman laughed too loud, a sound like breaking glass. Lila’s engagement ring caught the low light. She twisted it off, slipped it into her evening bag.

A server skirted the crowd with a tray of scallops. Lila took one without looking up. The shellfish was cold, the meat tender. She chewed slowly, savoring the salt.

“You’re not drinking.”

Conrad Holloway III stood at the edge of her vision, hands loose in his pockets. His suit was charcoal, impeccable. His jaw was freshly shaved, the scent of sandalwood clinging to him.

Lila swallowed. “Not tonight.”

“You always drink champagne.” He slid onto the stool beside her. The barstool groaned under his weight. “Something to celebrate?”

She set the empty shell on a napkin. “A funeral, more like.”

His smile didn’t waver. “You’re in a mood.”

“Am I?” She tilted her head, just enough. “Or are you?”

Conrad signaled the bartender, pointed at her glass. The bartender poured without asking. Conrad lifted the new flute in a mock toast. “To silent partners.”

Lila’s fingers tightened around her stem. “You’re well-informed.”

“Always.” He took a sip. “Though I must admit, I didn’t see Tomas going down like this.”

She didn’t react. “Neither did I.”

Conrad leaned in. His cologne was expensive. Too expensive. “But you knew about the ledger.”

Her pulse didn’t jump. Her breath didn’t hitch. She picked up the fresh champagne and drank, letting the bubbles burn her throat.

“Ledger?” she said, setting the glass down. “I’ve seen a lot of ledgers lately.”

Conrad’s eyes flicked to the bartender, who had drifted toward the far end of the bar. He lowered his voice. “The one that proves you’ve been funding every project I thought was mine.”

Lila laughed. A real sound. “You think I care about your projects?”

“I think,” he said, voice low and smooth as oil, “you care about power. Control. The kind only money can buy.”

She met his gaze. Held it. “And yet, here you are, chasing a ghost.”

Conrad’s fingers tapped the bar. A nervous habit. One she’d never seen before. “I know what you did to El Estero.”

“What I *did*?” She raised an eyebrow. “I bought the debt. Saved the wetlands. Something you’d never consider.”

“Something I’d never *need* to consider.” His voice dropped to a growl. “You think you’re clever, Lila. But clever girls get caught.”

She leaned back. “Are you threatening me, Conrad?”

He didn’t answer. Just stared at her, his pale eyes unreadable.

Lila reached for her bag. “I should go.”

“Wait.” He caught her wrist. Not hard. Just firm enough. “Where’s the ledger?”

She looked at his hand. Then at him. “I have no idea what you’re talking about.”

Conrad released her. Slowly. Deliberately. “You always were a terrible liar.”

She stood. The champagne flute wobbled, nearly toppled. She steadied it, then tucked her bag under her arm.

“Enjoy your evening, Conrad,” she said. “Though I suspect it’s about to get much worse.”

He didn’t move as she walked away. Didn’t call after her. Didn’t try to stop her.

The lodge lobby was quiet, the marble floors slick under her heels. She passed a framed photo of the Lodge’s grand opening, Conrad shaking hands with the mayor. In the photo, Lila stood beside her mother, both of them smiling.

She didn’t look at the photo. Didn’t need to.

At the elevator, she pressed the button. The doors slid open immediately.

Inside, she leaned against the mirrored wall. Her reflection stared back—pale, composed, untouchable.

The elevator descended. She exhaled.

Then her phone buzzed.

Unknown number. Text only: *They’re reversing the vote. Harbor Commission just voted in favor of the project.*

She stared at the screen. Her thumb hovered over the delete button.

Before she could decide, the elevator dinged. The doors opened.

A concierge stood there, holding a silver tray. “Ms. Cortez? A package for you.”

She took it. No return address. Just her name in neat script.

Inside was a single envelope. No note. Only a flash drive.

She palmed it, slipped it into her bag.

The concierge stepped back. “Will there be anything else?”

“No,” she said. “Thank you.”

She walked out into the cool night air. The harbor lights glimmered on the water, reflecting the stars. The scent of brine and gasoline filled her lungs.

Her phone buzzed again. This time, a call.

Conrad’s number.

She declined it. Slid the phone into her pocket.

A car idled at the curb—a black town car, tinted windows. She didn’t get in.

Instead, she walked toward the water, her heels clicking on the pavement. The tide was high, lapping at the seawall.

She stopped at the edge, where the wet concrete met the restless water. The flash drive was cool and smooth in her palm.

She didn’t plug it in. Didn’t need to.

She knew what was on it. Diego’s voice, clear and steady: *“Tomas admits to embezzling from Lila Voss’s estate. Claims she redirected Holloway funds to local projects. Says she owns the shell companies outright.”*

Her breath fogged in the cold air.

Behind her, footsteps echoed on the pavement.

She didn’t turn. Didn’t need to.

She knew who it was.

“You’re late,” she said.

Diego stepped beside her. His hands were shoved deep in his pockets. His face was shadowed in the dim light. “Conrad’s men were at the Coast Guard station.”

“You lost them?”

“No.” He looked at her. Really looked. “They lost me.”

She exhaled, slow and steady. “What’s on the drive?”

“Everything.” He pulled a second flash drive from his pocket, held it up. “Tomas’s confession. The ledger pages. The shell companies. Even the recordings of Conrad admitting to fraud.”

She took it, turned it in her fingers. “Why did you send it to me?”

“Because you’re the only one who can use it.” He stepped closer. His shoulder brushed hers. “Conrad’s going to come for you tonight. Not tomorrow. Not next week. Tonight.”

She tucked both drives into her bag, zipped it shut. “He already tried.”

“He’s not going to stop.” Diego’s voice was quiet. “Not until he has the flash drives. Not until he’s destroyed the ledger.”

She looked at him. Really looked. The dark circles under his eyes. The tension in his jaw. The way his fingers twitched at his sides, like he was fighting the urge to reach for her.

“You’re shaking,” she said.

He didn’t deny it. “I’m cold.”

She unzipped her bag, pulled out her wrap, tossed it to him. He caught it, draped it over his shoulders. The wool swallowed him.

“Better?”

He nodded. “Thanks.”

She zipped her bag shut. “What’s the play?”

“Conrad will expect you to run. Hide. Protect yourself.” Diego’s voice was low, urgent. “But you’re not going to do that.”

“No,” she said. “I’m not.”

He stepped closer. Close enough that she could feel the heat of him through the fabric of her dress. Close enough that she could smell the sea and salt on his skin.

“Lila,” he said. Her name sounded like a prayer.

She looked up at him. “What?”

He didn’t answer. Not with words. Instead, he reached up, brushed his thumb along her jawline. A featherlight touch. Gone before she could flinch.

“You always did know how to burn a boat,” he said. “But can you burn an empire?”

She didn’t answer. Just stared at him, her breath shallow.

A gust of wind howled off the water, rattling the seawall. The tide surged higher, lapping at her heels.

Diego stepped back. Put distance between them. “Conrad’s coming. And he’s not alone.”

She looked toward the lodge. Shadows moved near the entrance. Dark suits. Straight lines. They fanned out, scanning the area.

“What do we do?” she asked.

Diego pulled out his phone, tapped the screen. A text glowed in the dim light: *They’re here.*

He looked at her. Really looked. “We make sure Conrad Holloway III never sees tomorrow.”

She didn’t ask how. Didn’t need to.

She knew.

She pulled out her phone. Texted one word to an untraceable number: *Now.*

Diego’s phone buzzed. He read the message, then looked at her. “They’re in position.”

She took his hand. Squeezed it. Once. Firm.

“Then let’s go,” she said.

They walked toward the lodge together, shoulders brushing, fingers laced.

The shadows moved toward them, faster now.

Lila didn’t flinch. Didn’t slow.

She was done running.

chapters.title: **Chapter 12: The Unmasking**
chapters.text:
**Chapter 12: The Unmasking**

The empty ballroom of the Monterey Bay Lodge smelled of lemon wax and old roses. The grand piano stood like a relic against the far wall, its lid closed, the keys dulled under years of neglect. Lila ran her fingers along the wood grain of the instrument, tracing the same initials her mother had carved into the bench decades ago.

Conrad Holloway III stood in the center of the room, his reflection stretched long across the polished floor. He wore a suit the color of wet slate, his cufflinks flashing silver in the low light. When he turned, his smile wasn’t for her.

“You called,” he said. “I wasn’t sure you wanted to see me again after our last chat at the bar.”

Lila didn’t answer. She walked to the piano, lifted the lid. The hinges creaked. Inside, the ledger waited, its pages crisp and new.

Conrad’s smile flickered. “What is that?”

She didn’t look at him. “Proof.”

He stepped closer. The soles of his shoes clicked against the marble. “Proof of what?”

She flipped the ledger open, pressed her palm against the page. “Of everything.”

Conrad leaned in, scanning the columns. His breath hitched. A muscle in his jaw jumped. “What the hell is this?”

“Your empire,” she said. “Or what’s left of it.”
